graph processing
  • Home

implementation

Implementation of graph, index, and calculation relationships.

  • An indexRange manages the index content of one dimension of one variable.

    • This is implemented as a list, with attributes for class and rangeType
    • rangeTypes include sequence (1:10), matrix (arbitrary), scalar, and blank.

  • An indexRule manages the determination of LHS indices from RHS indices for one or more indexRanges. (Sometimes multiple index dimensions needs to be solved jointly.)

    • Kinds of indexRules include block and arbitrary.
    • Inputs and outputs to application of an indexRule are indexRanges.
    • Inputs that are out of range do not produce output
    • Includes checks for going out of range
    • By reversing the order of a declaration, we can create both parent and child rules
    • Consider all possibilities of LHS blank, constant, vector/block and RHS blank, constant, vector/block.

  • An originalIndexRule manages the determination of nodeFunction indices from actual index values, e.g., with a declaration such as y[i+1] <- x[i], determining that calculation for y[2] is handled with i=1.

    • This can be handled with the indexRule functionality by making dummy declarations such as w[i,j] <- y[i+j] if the focal declaration is, say, y[i+j] <- x[i] + x[j].

  • A varRange manages a set of indexRanges representing all dimensions of a variable.

    • A varRange also keeps track of how each indexRange would be crossed or combined with others to represent the set of variable elements correctly.
    • Ultimately we may want to be able to collapse multiple varRanges to a single varRange when possible.

  • A graphRule manages the set of indexRules needed to determine all LHS indices from all RHS indices for one set of edges created one of the RHS variables and the LHS variable of a single declaration.

    • Inputs and outputs to a graphRule are varRanges.
    • Probably retain stoch vs. det indication via pointer to relevant declRule to help with dependency queries.

  • A varRules manages the set of graphRules for the same variable (RHS).

  • A calcRange manages the indexing for a single calculation for a variable

    • Keeps track of declaration (via declRule, including associated nodeFunction), indexing (using varRanges), index values, and nodeFunction version to be used.
    • Index values are based on original declaration context, e.g., a declaration of y[i+1] ~ dnorm(x[i], 1) with i=1:5 has calcRange indexing of 1:5 since nodeFunctions use i directly.
    • Includes sort ID so that multiple calcRanges can be calculated in correct order.
    • Eventually might deal with doing calcs that don’t have complete sort ties, e.g., y[i]~dfoo(y[i-1]), as a block but probably not to start.

  • There will be multiple nodeFunctions for a given declaration, allowing for various types of vectorized calculations.

    • Still a bit up in the air – currently calcRange has a calculation function that loops over ranges of original indexes and calls declRule scalar-index-valued nodeFunction.
    • Currently unclear how the compilation of this would work.

  • A calcRule manages a single varRange that can be calculated jointly.

    • Inherits from the nodeRule base class (see below) as we also need to be able to determine nodeRanges containing elements of all the same type (e.g., from getNodeNames(latent = TRUE).
    • An individual calcRule is produced from the elements in a declaration that are tied in terms of their sort order and (are all of the same type).
    • Includes parent/child links.
    • Associated with types such as ‘end’,‘top’, etc. since those will relate directly to sort ID. (Noting that theta[i] ~ dnorm(theta0[i], 1) are not necessarily all latent or all top nodes, as it depends on how theta0[i] are declared; this would involve splitting an original calcRule when graph processing is done.)
    • This avoids having to store information in variableStores and can use all of our tools for working with indexRanges and varRanges.
    • When passed a varRange (or a nodeRange) it returns a calcRange (or nothing if there is no overlap between the input varRange and the varRange of the rule) that can be used for calculation.
    • Uses originalIndexRules from its declRule to relate back to original index values in the declaration context.
      • Indexing is based on indexes of the variable, not the original index values, e.g., y[i+1] ~ dnorm(x[i], 1) with i=1:5 has the varRange of y[2:6].
    • Contains the sort ID for the varRange.
    • Produced from original calcRule generated by each declRule and subsequent fracturing of those rules (using fracture()) based on walking down the graph.
    • Inherits density calculation from original nodeRule it is created from.
    • Indexing is over nodes.
    • everything on LHS starts out as both top and end
    • top found by walking down the graph (from known top [those with only constant RHS] and RHSonly), fracturing, and tagging what has stoch parents; the resulting calcRules that don’t have stoch parents are top
    • end found by starting with final calcRules with no dependents. Then also walk up from each stoch end rule and anything found along the way is removed from being end
    • latent found by what is not top or end
    • sort ID presumably determined by recursion based on walking the final set of calcRules (which will have parent/child information) either top-down or bottom-up (perhaps bottom-up so that all data nodes are set to be tied, given data nodes generally more numerous than parameter nodes, but maybe not if thinking about nodeRanges that group many current ‘nodes’.

  • A varCalcRules manages the set of calcRules for a given variable.

    • varTopRules, varEndRules, varStochRules, etc. manage the set of calcRuless for a given type and variable.

  • A nodeRule manages a single set of nodes from a declaration that are equivalent in some sense. Given a varRange, it produces a nodeRange (or nothing if no overlap).

    • A base class for declRule, calcRule, and RHSrule
    • Input is a varRange.
    • Output is a nodeRange.
    • Indexing is over nodes (see comments below regarding nodeRange).

AdeclRulemanages information from a single declaration - Generates the nodeFunction as function of scalar-valued index arguments (e.g.,calculate(i,j)) - ContainsoriginalIndexRulewith indexing information needed for calculations to pass along to relatedcalcRules. - Created by initial pass through model, with LHS from the declarations - Notion of "equivalence" in terms of sort order, type, etc. is pushed down intocalcRules. - ContainsoriginalIndexRuleto pass along to constituentcalcRule`s.

ArhsRulemanages information about RHS only nodes - Produced from originalrhsRules generated from all RHS terms in a declaration and subsequent modification of those rules based on all or portions of the RHS appearing in a different LHS rule (acalcRule) viaexclude()`.

  • A nodeRange compactly represents a set of equivalent nodes

    • E.g., y[i, 1:3] for i in 1:500 or y[i, j] for i in 1:5, j in 1:20
    • Not equivalent to a varRange as y[i, 1:3] for i in 1:500 is a nodeRange while y[1:500, 1:3] is a varRange
      • Contains the internalRange (e.g., 1:3) and the nodeRange (e.g, 1:500); probably want a different name for the embedded ‘nodeRange’
    • Currently contains a getVarRange method that converts to the equivalent varRange.
    • Perhaps printed representation is y[1:500, (1:3)], where (..) represents the ‘internal’ block.
    • Perhaps should be re-implemented as inheriting from a varRange as various API calls should work with either varRange or nodeRange input (e.g., getDependencies, getNodes).
      • internalRange and externalRange currently would need to be varRanges (and have redundant info to the indexRanges), but perhaps some way to rewor so they are subsets of indexRanges.

  • A copyRule manages the determination of copying for a variable

    • presumably trivial but a bit fuzzy at the moment
    • Input is a varRange?
    • Output is a varRange.
    • Perhaps all it needs to do is check for out of bounds and expand missing indexes?

  • (Deprecated?) A variableStore manages array-like information for variables (see below for alternative idea of using varTypeRules)

    • Stores information such as sort ID, isData, node types
    • Could be integer, logical, or character-valued
    • Should efficiently handle case where all values are the same
    • Ultimately may want to handle additional structure, such as blocks of equal values
      • Could have sets of blocks, with remaining values a “discontinuous block” if all the same.
    • Ultimately may want to handle simple sequential values (e.g., row-major or column-major in a matrix) as this could be useful for sort ID values in state-space/time-series contexts.
    • Should we have variableStores that map back to calcRules and nodeRules? This might avoid need to store something like node type if all node types for a given nodeRule are the same. Also avoids need for variable-level sort ID unless there is an ordering with a given calcRule (since could just look up sort ID of the calcRule).

  • Topological sorting needs to:

    • create a sort ID for each calcRule
    • These sort IDs can be generated from the final calcRules as being the minimum of the sort IDs of the childen calcRules minus 1, using recursion. (Alternatively, go from top down with maximum of sort IDs of parents plus 1.)
    • still need to consider state-space type case where might want to have some sort of nested sort ID for a given calcRule.

  • Dynamic indexing still needs consideration.

    • Currently we introduce a hidden ‘unknownIndex’ lifted node that allows for our standard dependency processing.
    • Possibly handle things similarly to current approach: have one graphRule with varRange of the ‘unknownIndex’ as the LHS and another graphRule with varRange of the ‘unknownIndex’ as the RHS.
